1	\chapter{Flattening Stratification of a Coherent Sheaf}
2	\label{chap:Picard_FlatteningStratification}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean
4	
5	
6	\section{Setup and motivation}
7	\label{sec:flatstrat_setup}
8	
9	Generic flatness is the foundational input to the flattening-stratification
10	construction underlying Grothendieck's existence theorem for the Quot scheme
11	(\cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}, \S4--\S5).  Its content is that flatness of a
12	coherent sheaf is a generic property over an integral base: over a noetherian
13	integral scheme \(S\), a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module on a finite-type morphism
14	\(X \to S\) becomes flat after restricting to a non-empty open subscheme of
15	\(S\). Algebraically this is the statement that a finite module over a
16	finite-type algebra over a noetherian domain becomes free after inverting a
17	single non-zero element of the base ring.
18	
19	We work throughout with a noetherian (integral, where required) base scheme
20	\(S\), a finite-type morphism \(p : X \to S\), and a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module
21	\(\F\).
22	
23	\section{Generic flatness}
24	\label{sec:generic_flatness}
25	
26	The construction of the flattening stratification proceeds by Noetherian
27	induction on \(S\). The induction step relies on the following classical
28	\emph{generic flatness} theorem of Grothendieck: over a noetherian integral
29	base, a coherent sheaf is flat over a non-empty open subscheme. Algebraically,
30	this is the assertion that a finitely generated module over a finite-type
31	algebra over a noetherian domain is generically free after inverting a single
32	non-zero element of the base ring.
33	
34	\begin{theorem}
35	
36	
37	
38	\leanok
39	[Generic flatness, algebraic form]
40	  \label{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}
41	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatnessAlgebraic}
42	  \uses{lem:gf_torsion_base, lem:noeth_prime_filtration, lem:gf_splice_shortExact,
43	        lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators, lem:gf_polynomial_core, lem:gf_finite_module}
44	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''
45	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1711-L1716).
46	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
47	  % "Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain,
48	  %  and \(B\) a finite type \(A\) algebra. Let \(M\) be a finite \(B\)-module.
49	  %  Then there exists an \(f\in A\), \(f\ne 0\), such that
50	  %  the localisation \(M_f\) is a free module over \(A_f\)."
51	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''.}
52	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(B\) a finite-type \(A\)-algebra, and \(M\) a finite
53	  \(B\)-module. Then there exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that the localisation
54	  \(M_f\) is a free \(A_f\)-module.
55	
56	\end{theorem}
57	
58	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Over the quotient field \(K\) of \(A\), the \(K\)-algebra
59	% \(B_K = K\otimes _AB\) is of finite type, and \(M_K = K\otimes_AM\) is a
60	% finite module over \(B_K\). Let \(n\) be the dimension of the support
61	% of \(M_K\) over \(\spec B_K\). We argue by induction on \(n\), starting
62	% with \(n=-1\) which is the case when \(M_K =0\). [...]
63	% Now let \(n\ge 0\), and let the lemma be proved for smaller values.
64	% As \(B\) is noetherian and \(M\) is assumed to be a finite \(B\)-module,
65	% there exists a finite filtration
66	% \(0=M_0 \subset \ldots  \subset M_r =M\)
67	% where each \(M_i\) is a \(B\)-submodule of \(M\) such that for each \(i\ge 1\)
68	% the quotient module \(M_i/M_{i-1}\) is isomorphic to \(B/\pp_i\) for
69	% some prime ideal \(\pp_i\) in \(B\). [...] By Noether normalisation lemma,
70	% there exist elements \(b_1,\ldots,b_n \in B\), such that \(K\otimes_AB\) is
71	% finite over its subalgebra \(K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]\) [...]"
72	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1719-L1772; full
73	% proof omitted for length, structural steps restated below in project notation.)
74	\begin{proof}
75	
76	  \textbf{Primary route — the finite \(A\)-module case.}
77	  Suppose first that \(M\) is module-finite over \(A\) itself. Since \(A\) is noetherian,
78	  \(M\) is finitely presented over \(A\). The generic fibre \(M_K = K \otimes_A M\)
79	  (where \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\)) is a finite-dimensional \(K\)-vector space, hence free
80	  over \(K\); by \cref{lem:gf_finite_module} this freeness descends to a single basic open:
81	  there exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), with \(M_f\) free over \(A_f\). This closes the
82	  theorem whenever \(M\) is finite over \(A\).
83	
84	  \textbf{Surviving residue — reducing the finite-type \(B\)-algebra case to the
85	  finite \(A\)-module case.} The genuine content not supplied by Mathlib is the
86	  reduction of the general hypothesis (\(M\) finite over a \emph{finite-type}
87	  \(A\)-algebra \(B\)) to the finite \(A\)-module situation handled above. Concretely,
88	  the missing piece is the polynomial-ring core of generic freeness: a finite module
89	  over \(A[b_1, \dots, b_n]\) becomes free over \(A_f\) after inverting a single
90	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\). The classical \S4 dévissage below is the fallback route
91	  for exactly this residue.
92	
93	  \emph{Fallback (Nitsure \S4 induction).}
94	  The \S4 dévissage is assembled from the sub-lemmas of
95	  \cref{sec:gf_devissage_chain}. If \(M_K = 0\), the torsion base case
96	  \cref{lem:gf_torsion_base} gives \(f \ne 0\) with \(M_f = 0\) free. Otherwise the
97	  prime-filtration induction principle \cref{lem:noeth_prime_filtration} over the
98	  noetherian ring \(B\), combined with the splicing fact
99	  \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact}, reduces to \(M = B/\mathfrak{p}\) and hence to
100	  \(B\) a domain, \(M = B\); Noether normalisation with clearing of denominators
101	  \cref{lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators} makes \(B_g\) finite over a polynomial
102	  ring \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\), and the polynomial-ring core
103	  \cref{lem:gf_polynomial_core} — bottoming out at the finite-\(A\)-module leaf
104	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_module} of the primary route — produces the required
105	  \(f \ne 0\) with \(M_f\) free over \(A_f\).
106	\end{proof}

108	\subsection{Dévissage chain for the finite-type residue}
109	\label{sec:gf_devissage_chain}
110	
111	The following sub-lemmas decompose the surviving residue of
112	\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic} — the case where \(M\) is finite over the
113	finite-type \(A\)-algebra \(B\) but not module-finite over \(A\) — into the
114	classical \cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}~\S4 induction. They are ordered so that each block
115	depends only on earlier blocks or Mathlib lemmas. Throughout,
116	\(A\) is a noetherian domain with fraction field \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\), \(B\) a
117	finite-type \(A\)-algebra, and \(M\) a finite \(B\)-module with the compatible
118	\(A\)-module structure via the scalar tower \(A \to B \to M\).
119	
120	\begin{lemma}\leanok
121	[Finite-module leaf — finite \(A\)-module case]
122	  \label{lem:gf_finite_module}
123	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_of_finite}
124	  \uses{lem:fp_free_descent}
125	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain and \(M\) a finite \(A\)-module. Then there
126	  exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
127	\end{lemma}
128	
129	\begin{proof}
130	
131	  This is the \(d = 0\) leaf of the induction. A finite module over a noetherian ring is
132	  finitely presented, so
133	  \(M\) is finitely presented over \(A\). Over the generic point
134	  \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A) = S^{-1}A\) (\(S = A \setminus \{0\}\)) the localisation
135	  \(M_K = S^{-1}M\) is a finite-dimensional \(K\)-vector space, hence free over
136	  \(K\). The finite-presentation free-descent lemma \cref{lem:fp_free_descent}
137	  then descends this freeness to a single basic open: there is \(r \in S\), i.e.\
138	  \(r \in A\) with \(r \ne 0\), such that \(M_r\) is free over \(A_r\). Take
139	  \(f := r\).
140	\end{proof}

193	  cyclic quotient \(B/\mathfrak{p}\) with \(\mathfrak{p}\) prime, and (iii) is

224	\begin{lemma}\leanok
225	[L3b — free transport across a finer localisation]
226	  \label{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport}
227	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.free_localizationAway_of_free_of_eq_mul}
228	  Let \(N\) be a \(B\)-module and \(f', f'' \in A\) with \(f = f' f''\). If the
229	  localisation \(N_{f'}\) is free over \(A_{f'}\), then the further localisation
230	  \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
231	\end{lemma}
232	
233	\begin{proof}
234	
235	  Because \(f = f' f''\), the localisation away from \(f\) factors through the
236	  localisation away from \(f'\): the ring \(A_f\) is the localisation of \(A_{f'}\)
237	  at the image of \(f''\), and likewise the module \(N_f\) is the localisation of
238	  \(N_{f'}\) at the image of \(f''\). A localisation of a free module is free over
239	  the localised base ring — a basis of \(N_{f'}\) over \(A_{f'}\) maps to a basis of
240	  \(N_f\) over \(A_f\). Hence \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\). Stated as a standalone
241	  fact, it is applied to both ends of the spliced sequence.
242	\end{proof}

256	  short exact sequence splits: \(M_f \cong M'_f \oplus M''_f\). A finite direct sum
257	  of free \(A_f\)-modules is free, hence \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
258	\end{proof}

384	\begin{proof}
385	  \uses{lem:noether_normalization_fg, lem:gf_clear_one_denominator}
386	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1747-L1758).
387	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (Noether normalisation with denominators).}
388	  \emph{Step 1 — Noether normalisation over \(K\).} Apply Noether normalisation over the
389	  field \(K\) (\cref{lem:noether_normalization_fg}) to the finite-type \(K\)-algebra
390	  \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\): there are algebraically independent
391	  \(\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n \in B_K\) with \(B_K\) module-finite over
392	  \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). Clearing denominators, each \(\bar b_j\) is
393	  represented by some \(b_j \in B\) (writing \(1 \otimes b\) as \(b\)).
394	
395	  \emph{Step 2 — clear the integral-dependence denominators to a single \(g\).} Fix a
396	  finite set \(\sigma\) of algebra generators of \(B_K\) over
397	  \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). By Step 1 each generator's image
398	  \(1 \otimes x \in B_K\) is integral over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). For each
399	  \(x \in \sigma\), a standard integrality-and-localization descent argument yields a
400	  non-zero \(a_x \in A\) such that \(a_x \cdot (1 \otimes x)\)
401	  is integral over \(A[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). Multiplying over the finite
402	  set, set \(g_1 := \prod_{x \in \sigma} a_x\) and \(g := g_0 \cdot g_1\) (with \(g_0\) the
403	  Step-1 denominator witness). After inverting \(g\), every chosen generator becomes
404	  integral over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\) and the generators generate \(B_g\) as an algebra;
405	  hence \(B_g\) is module-finite over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). (The single-coefficient
406	  lemma \cref{lem:gf_clear_one_denominator} provides the same denominator-clearing primitive
407	  at the level of one coefficient polynomial; the proof discharges a whole generator
408	  at once using this integrality-descent step, folding that primitive over the coefficients
409	  internally.)

412	  is the \(A_g\)-algebra map \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to B_g\) sending \(X_j \mapsto b_j\),
413	  where \(b_j \in B_g\) is the image of \(b_j \in B\). We establish injectivity and
414	  module-finiteness through a comparison with the situation over \(K\).
415	
416	  \emph{Step 3a — the comparison map \(\nu : B_g \to B_K\).} Since \(g \ne 0\) in the
417	  domain \(A\) and \(A \subseteq K\), the image of \(g\) is a unit of the field \(K\), so
418	  it is invertible in \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\). Hence the canonical \(A\)-algebra map
419	  \(B \to B_K\), \(b \mapsto 1 \otimes b\), sends \(g\) to a unit and, by the universal
420	  property of localisation, factors through a canonical \(A\)-algebra map
421	  \[
422	    \nu : B_g = B[g^{-1}] \longrightarrow B_K, \qquad \nu(b/g^k) = (1 \otimes b)\,(1 \otimes g)^{-k}.
423	  \]
424	  The map \(\nu\) is injective. Indeed, \(B\) is a domain, and both \(B_g\) and \(B_K\) are
425	  localisations of \(B\) at multiplicative subsets of the non-zerodivisors: \(B_g\) inverts
426	  the powers of \(g\), while \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\) inverts (in particular) the images of
427	  all non-zero elements of \(A\), a multiplicative set containing the powers of \(g\). A
428	  localisation of a domain at a set of non-zerodivisors is injective into any further such
429	  localisation. Concretely, the natural map \(B \to B_K\) is injective on the domain \(B\)
430	  (its kernel is the set of \(b\) annihilated by some non-zero element of \(A\), which in a
431	  domain over which \(A\) acts faithfully is \(0\)), so \(\nu(b/g^k) = 0\) forces
432	  \(1 \otimes b = 0\), hence \(b = 0\) and \(b/g^k = 0\). Under \(\nu\) the lifts
433	  \(b_j \in B_g\) of the Noether generators map to the elements \(\bar b_j \in B_K\) of
434	  Step 1: \(\nu(b_j) = \bar b_j\).
435	
436	  \emph{Step 3b — injectivity of \(\varphi\) by algebraic-independence descent
437	  \(K \to A_g\).} Injectivity of the \(A_g\)-algebra map
438	  \(\varphi : A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to B_g\), \(X_j \mapsto b_j\), is equivalent to the
439	  algebraic independence of the family \((b_j)\) over \(A_g\): a non-zero element of the
440	  kernel is exactly a non-trivial \(A_g\)-polynomial relation \(P(b_1, \dots, b_n) = 0\).
441	  The localisation \(A_g = A[g^{-1}] \subseteq K\) embeds in the fraction field, giving an
442	  injection of \(A_g\)-algebras \(A_g \hookrightarrow K\). Now suppose
443	  \(P \in A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) is non-zero with \(P(b_1, \dots, b_n) = 0\) in \(B_g\).
444	  Applying the injective map \(\nu\) and using \(\nu(b_j) = \bar b_j\) together with the fact
445	  that \(\nu\) is an \(A\)-algebra map extending scalars to \(K\), we obtain
446	  \(P^K(\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n) = 0\) in \(B_K\), where \(P^K\) is the image of \(P\)
447	  under the coefficient inclusion \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \hookrightarrow K[X_1, \dots, X_n]\).
448	  Since \(A_g \hookrightarrow K\) is injective, \(P \ne 0\) gives \(P^K \ne 0\); but the
449	  \(\bar b_j\) are algebraically independent over \(K\) (Step 1), so the only \(K\)-polynomial
450	  vanishing on them is \(0\), a contradiction. Hence no non-trivial relation exists, \((b_j)\)
451	  is algebraically independent over \(A_g\), and \(\varphi\) is injective.
452	
453	  \emph{Step 3c — module-finiteness of \(B_g\) over \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\).} By Step 1,
454	  \(B_K\) is module-finite over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\); fix a finite set of algebra
455	  generators of \(B_K\) over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\) (Step 2). Each such generator
456	  satisfies a monic equation of integral dependence whose coefficients are polynomials in
457	  \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). The element \(g\) of Step 2 was chosen as a common
458	  denominator for the (finitely many) coefficient polynomials of these equations, so that
459	  after inverting \(g\) every such coefficient already lies in
460	  \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n] = \operatorname{im}\varphi\). Each chosen generator is the
461	  \(\nu\)-image of an element of \(B_g\), and its integral-dependence equation is the
462	  \(\nu\)-image of the corresponding monic equation holding in \(B_g\) with coefficients in
463	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\); since \(\nu\) is injective, that equation holds in \(B_g\)
464	  itself. Thus each generator of \(B_g\) is integral over the subalgebra
465	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\), and these generators generate \(B_g\) as an
466	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\)-algebra. A finite-type algebra that is integral over a
467	  subalgebra is module-finite over it; hence \(B_g\) is module-finite over
468	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi = A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). Transported along the injection
469	  \(\varphi\), this is exactly module-finiteness of \(B_g\) over \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) for
470	  the algebra structure that \(\varphi\) puts on \(B_g\), as claimed. This recovers the
471	  source's conclusion that \(B_g\) is finite over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\).
472	\end{proof}

474	\begin{lemma}\leanok
475	[injectivity of a localisation lift]
476	  \label{lem:gf_isLocalization_lift_injective}
477	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.isLocalization_lift_injective}
478	  Let \(S\) be a localisation of a ring \(R\) at a multiplicative set \(M\), and let
479	  \(g : R \to P\) be a ring homomorphism sending every element of \(M\) to a unit of
480	  \(P\), so that it induces the lift \(\widehat g : S \to P\). If both the localisation
481	  map \(R \to S\) and \(g\) itself are injective, then \(\widehat g\) is injective.
482	\end{lemma}
483	\begin{proof}
484	  The lift is characterised by \(\widehat g(r/m) = g(r)\,g(m)^{-1}\). By the universal
485	  criterion for injectivity of a localisation lift, \(\widehat g\) is injective iff for
486	  all \(r_1, r_2 \in R\) the equality of localisation images
487	  \((r_1 \mapsto S) = (r_2 \mapsto S)\) implies \(g(r_1) = g(r_2)\). Both the hypothesis
488	  and the conclusion of that implication reduce, via injectivity of \(R \to S\) and of
489	  \(g\), to the single condition \(r_1 = r_2\); hence the implication holds and
490	  \(\widehat g\) is injective. (This helper is applied twice in
491	  \cref{lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators}, to the comparison maps \(\nu : B_g \to B_K\)
492	  and \(\psi : A_g \to K\).)
493	\end{proof}

495	\begin{lemma}
496	
497	
498	\leanok
499	[L5a — the generic-rank short exact sequence]
500	  \label{lem:gf_generic_rank_ses}
501	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_generic_rank_ses}
502	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
503	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1760-L1765).
504	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
505	  % "Let $m$ be the generic rank of the finite module $B_g$ over the
506	  %  domain $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$. Then we have a short exact
507	  %  sequence of $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$-modules of the form
508	  %  $$0\to A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]^{\oplus m} \to B_g \to T \to 0$$
509	  %  where $T$ is a finite torsion module over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$."
510	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (generic-rank SES).}
511	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(d \ge 0\), and \(N\) a finite module over the
512	  polynomial ring \(P_d := A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) (with its induced \(A\)-module structure
513	  through the scalar tower \(A \to P_d \to N\)). Then there exist \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) and
514	  an injective \(P_d\)-linear map \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) whose cokernel
515	  \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\) is a torsion \(P_d\)-module. Equivalently, there is
516	  a short exact sequence of \(P_d\)-modules
517	  \[
518	    0 \to P_d^{\oplus m} \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } N \to T \to 0,
519	    \qquad T \text{ torsion}.
520	  \]
521	
522	  The integer \(m\) is the \emph{generic rank} of \(N\): the dimension of the
523	  \(\mathrm{Frac}(P_d)\)-vector space
524	  \(\mathrm{Frac}(P_d) \otimes_{P_d} N\), i.e.\ of the localisation of \(N\) at the
525	  non-zero-divisors of the domain \(P_d\). This step lives over \(P_d\) directly —
526	  \emph{no inversion of any element \(g \in A\) is required here}; the element \(g\) of
527	  Nitsure's argument enters only at the reindexing step \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex}.
528	\end{lemma}
529	
530	\begin{proof}
531	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1760-L1765).
532	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (generic-rank SES).}
533	  Write \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\); since \(A\) is a noetherian domain, so is \(P_d\)
534	  (Hilbert basis theorem, and a polynomial ring over a domain is a domain). Let
535	  \(Q = \mathrm{Frac}(P_d)\) be its fraction field and
536	  \(N_Q := Q \otimes_{P_d} N\) the localisation of \(N\) at the non-zero-divisors of
537	  \(P_d\); as \(N\) is finite over \(P_d\), \(N_Q\) is a finite-dimensional
538	  \(Q\)-vector space. Put \(m := \dim_Q N_Q\), the generic rank.
539	
540	  Choose a \(Q\)-basis of \(N_Q\) and clear denominators: each basis vector is a
541	  \(Q\)-unit multiple of the image of some \(v_i \in N\), so the images of
542	  \(v_1, \dots, v_m \in N\) again form a \(Q\)-basis of \(N_Q\). In particular the family
543	  \((v_i)_{i}\) is linearly independent over \(Q\); restricting scalars along the
544	  injection \(P_d \hookrightarrow Q\), it is linearly independent over \(P_d\). Let
545	  \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) be the \(P_d\)-linear map
546	  \((c_1, \dots, c_m) \mapsto \sum_i c_i v_i\). Linear independence of \((v_i)\) over
547	  \(P_d\) makes \(\varphi\) injective.
548	
549	  Set \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\). Because the images of \(v_1, \dots, v_m\)
550	  already span \(N_Q\) over \(Q\), the localised map \(\varphi \otimes Q : Q^{\oplus m}
551	  \to N_Q\) is an isomorphism, so \(Q \otimes_{P_d} T = 0\). A finite \(P_d\)-module whose
552	  localisation at the non-zero-divisors of \(P_d\) vanishes is torsion; hence \(T\) is a
553	  torsion \(P_d\)-module. This is the asserted short exact sequence.
554	\end{proof}

575	[L5b.1 — annihilator extraction for the torsion module]
576	  \label{lem:gf_torsion_annihilator}
577	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_torsion_annihilator}
578	  \uses{lem:annihilator_meets_nonZeroDivisors}

581	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
582	  % "each $v\in M$ is annihilated by some non-zero element of $A$. Taking a finite
583	  %  generating set, and a common multiple of corresponding annihilating
584	  %  elements, we see there exists an $f\ne 0$ in $A$ with $fM=0$."
585	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (torsion annihilation).}

587	  over \(P_d := A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\). Then there exists a non-zero
588	  \(F \in \operatorname{Ann}_{P_d}(T)\); consequently the \(P_d\)-action on \(T\) factors
589	  through \(P_d/(F)\) and \(T\) is a finite module over \(P_d/(F)\).

593	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1724-L1727).
594	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (torsion annihilation).}

597	  \cref{lem:annihilator_meets_nonZeroDivisors} the annihilator of \(T\) meets the

602	  \(T\) generates \(T\) over \(P_d/(F)\), so \(T\) is finite over \(P_d/(F)\).

605	\subsubsection{Nagata change-of-variables machinery}
606	\label{sec:gf_nagata_machinery}
607	
608	The Nagata normalisation lemma \cref{lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar} is assembled from
609	technical helper lemmas implementing the triangular change of variables and the
610	radix-encoding degree bookkeeping, following the Nagata trick of
611	\cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}~\S4 over a noetherian domain. Throughout, \(k\) is a domain,
612	\(n \ge 0\), and \(f \in k[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) is a fixed polynomial; we write
613	\(\mathrm{up} := 2 + \deg_{\mathrm{tot}}(f)\) and the radix weights \(r_i := \mathrm{up}^{\,i}\)
614	for \(i = 0, \dots, n\) (so every exponent occurring in \(f\) is a base-\(\mathrm{up}\) digit).
615	The distinguished variable is \(X_0\).
616	
617	\begin{definition}\leanok
618	[Nagata substitution \(T_1\)]
619	  \label{def:gf_nagata_T1}
620	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.T1}
621	  \textit{Source: Nitsure \S4 (Nagata change of variables).}
622	  For a scalar \(c \in k\), the substitution \(T_1(c)\) is the \(k\)-algebra endomorphism
623	  of \(k[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) determined on generators by
624	  \[
625	    X_0 \mapsto X_0, \qquad X_i \mapsto X_i + c\,X_0^{\,r_i} \quad (i \ne 0).
626	  \]
627	  It is triangular in the variables \(X_i\) (\(i \ne 0\)) over the distinguished variable
628	  \(X_0\); this is the device realising the support-dimension drop.
629	\end{definition}
630	
631	\begin{lemma}\leanok
632	[\(T_1\) is invertible by the opposite shift]
633	  \label{lem:gf_t1_comp_t1_neg}
634	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.t1_comp_t1_neg}
635	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T1}
636	  For every \(c \in k\), \(T_1(c) \circ T_1(-c) = \mathrm{id}\); the Nagata substitution is
637	  invertible, with inverse the substitution of opposite coefficient.
638	\end{lemma}
639	\begin{proof}
640	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T1}
641	  It suffices to check the two composites agree on each generator: \(X_0 \mapsto X_0\), and
642	  \(X_i \mapsto X_i + (-c)X_0^{r_i} + c\,X_0^{r_i} = X_i\) for \(i \ne 0\).
643	\end{proof}

645	\begin{definition}\leanok
646	[Nagata automorphism \(T\)]
647	  \label{def:gf_nagata_T}
648	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.T}
649	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T1, lem:gf_t1_comp_t1_neg}
650	  \textit{Source: Nitsure \S4 (Nagata change of variables).}
651	  The Nagata change of variables is the \(k\)-algebra automorphism \(T := T_1(1)\) of

653	  \(X_0 \mapsto X_0\) — packaged as an algebra equivalence with inverse \(T_1(-1)\) via
654	  \cref{lem:gf_t1_comp_t1_neg}.
655	\end{definition}

707	[Distinct monomials acquire distinct \(X_0\)-degrees]

723	\begin{lemma}\leanok
724	[Leading coefficient of a transformed monomial]
725	  \label{lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
726	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}

731	\end{lemma}

739	\begin{lemma}\leanok
740	[Leading coefficient of \(T(f)\) is a non-zero constant]
741	  \label{lem:gf_T_leadingcoeff_eq}
742	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.T_leadingcoeff_eq}
743	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne, lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}

745	  the leading coefficient of \(T(f)\) in \(X_0\) equals the constant \(c_v := [X^v]\,f \ne 0\).
746	  Thus \(T(f)\) is, up to the non-zero scalar \(c_v\), monic in \(X_0\).
747	\end{lemma}
748	\begin{proof}
749	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne, lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
750	  Expand \(f = \sum_{w \in \mathrm{supp}\,f} c_w X^w\). By \cref{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne}
751	  the transformed monomials \(T(c_w X^w)\) have pairwise distinct \(X_0\)-degrees, so one of
752	  them — say at \(v\) — strictly dominates the others; the leading term of

788	  \label{lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar}

795	  % "Therefore, the dimension of the support of $K\otimes_{A_g}T$
796	  %  as a $K\otimes_{A_g}(B_g)$-module is strictly less than $n$. Hence by
797	  %  induction on $n$ (applied to the data $A_g$, $B_g$, $T$),"
798	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (support-dimension drop).}
799	  Let \(A\) be a domain, \(n \ge 0\), and \(0 \ne F \in A[X_0, \dots, X_n]\). Then there
800	  exist a triangular \(A\)-algebra automorphism \(e\) of \(A[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) and a
801	  non-zero \(g \in A\) such that, after inverting \(g\), the image of \(e(F)\) in
802	  \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_n]\), viewed as a univariate polynomial in \(X_0\) over
803	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\), has a \emph{unit} leading coefficient. Equivalently, \(e(F)\)
804	  is monic in \(X_0\) up to the unit \(g\) over \(A_g\).
805	\end{lemma}
806	
807	\begin{proof}
808	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1766-L1768).
809	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (support-dimension drop, Nagata normalisation).}
810	  Apply the Nagata substitution \(X_i \mapsto X_i + X_0^{r^{\,i}}\) for \(i = 1, \dots, n\)
811	  and \(X_0 \mapsto X_0\), with \(r\) larger than every exponent occurring in \(F\): it is a
812	  triangular (hence invertible) \(A\)-algebra automorphism \(e\) of \(A[X_0, \dots, X_n]\).
813	  Under this substitution the distinct monomials of \(F\) acquire distinct degrees in
814	  \(X_0\), so \(e(F)\), expanded as a polynomial in \(X_0\), has a unique top-degree term
815	  whose coefficient is the coefficient \(c \in A\) of the top total-degree monomial of
816	  \(F\); since \(F \ne 0\) this \(c \ne 0\). Put \(g := c\). The degree of \(e(F)\) in
817	  \(X_0\) equals the total degree of \(F\), and its leading coefficient in \(X_0\) is the
818	  constant \(g\). Passing to \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\), the image of \(g\) is a unit, so the
819	  image of \(e(F)\) in \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) is monic in \(X_0\) up to the unit \(g\).
820	\end{proof}

1	\chapter{The tautological quotient and the universal property of $\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)$}
2	\label{chap:Picard_GrassmannianQuot}
3	
4	\providecommand{\ShMod}{\mathrm{SheafOfModules}}
5	
6	The previous chapter constructed the Grassmannian scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over
7	\(\mathbb{Z}\) by gluing the affine charts \(U^I\)
8	(\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) along the transition isomorphisms
9	(\cref{def:gr_transition}) satisfying the cocycle condition
10	(\cref{lem:gr_cocycle}), and proved that the structure morphism
11	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is separated (\cref{lem:gr_separated})
12	and proper (\cref{lem:gr_proper}). This chapter adds the next layer: the
13	\emph{tautological rank-\(d\) quotient}
14	\(u : \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\,r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) of the
15	trivial rank-\(r\) sheaf, and the \emph{universal property} that makes
16	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) the fine moduli space of rank-\(d\) locally free quotients of
17	\(\mathcal{O}^{r}\) -- equivalently, the representing scheme of the Quot functor
18	of the trivial sheaf.
19	
20	Throughout, \(r \ge d \ge 1\) are the fixed integers of the previous chapter, and
21	for a size-\(d\) subset \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) we write \(R^I\) for the
22	chart ring \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) of \(U^I = \Spec R^I\) and \(R^I_J\) for the
23	localisation along \(P^I_J = \det(X^I_J)\) defining the overlap
24	\(U^I_J \subseteq U^I\).
25	
26	\section{Infrastructure: locally free sheaves and module gluing}
27	\label{sec:grquot_infra}
28	
29	The constructions of this chapter -- the universal quotient sheaf
30	\(\mathcal{U}\), the tautological quotient \(u\), and the functor of points -- rest
31	on two pieces of foundational machinery for sheaves of modules. The first is the
32	predicate ``locally free of rank \(d\)'' (\cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}),
33	which records that a sheaf of modules is, on the members of some open cover,
34	isomorphic to the trivial bundle \(\mathcal{O}^{d}\); it is the condition cut out
35	in the definition of the Grassmannian functor. That predicate is the project-local
36	\(\mathrm{SheafOfModules.IsLocallyFreeOfRank}\) already set up in the Quot-scheme
37	chapter (\cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}), which this chapter reuses verbatim;
38	the only foundational machinery genuinely new to this chapter is the second piece:
39	a gluing primitive that descends a sheaf of modules (and a morphism of such
40	sheaves) from per-chart data along an open cover, given a transition cocycle
41	compatible with the cover's own gluing; it is the vehicle that assembles the
42	per-chart free sheaves \(\mathcal{O}^{d}_{U^I}\) and the per-chart quotients
43	\(u^I\) into the global \(\mathcal{U}\) and \(u\), and is set up below.
44	
45	Stating the triple-overlap multiplicativity of the descent datum, however, first
46	requires a small piece of base-change machinery for the pullback functor on sheaves
47	of modules: the three transition isomorphisms attached to a triple of charts live a
48	priori on three different overlaps, and to compare their composite they must be
49	transported to a single common overlap. The next two blocks supply exactly this
50	transport -- the pseudofunctorial comparison of iterated pullbacks
51	(\cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}) and its packaging along the triple-overlap data of a
52	scheme glue datum (\cref{lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport}) -- after which the
53	cocycle condition can be expressed cleanly.
54	
55	\begin{definition}[Comparison of iterated pullbacks of sheaves of modules]
56	  \label{def:modules_pullbackComp}
57	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp}
58	  \mathlibok
59	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
60	  Let \(a : X \to Y\) and \(b : Y \to Z\) be morphisms of schemes. For a sheaf of
61	  \(\mathcal{O}_Z\)-modules \(\mathcal{M}\), pulling back first along \(b\) and then
62	  along \(a\) agrees with pulling back along the composite \(b \circ a\): there is a
63	  natural isomorphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
64	  \[
65	    a^{*}\bigl(b^{*}\mathcal{M}\bigr)
66	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
67	    (b \circ a)^{*}\mathcal{M},
68	  \]
69	  natural in \(\mathcal{M}\), expressing that \(\mathcal{M} \mapsto \mathcal{M}\)'s
70	  pullback is a pseudofunctor on schemes. These comparison isomorphisms satisfy the
71	  pentagon (associativity) coherence for a triple of composable morphisms. This is the
72	  pullback half of the pseudofunctoriality of sheaves of modules and is supplied by
73	  Mathlib.
74	\end{definition}
75	
76	\begin{lemma}[Base-change transport of a transition isomorphism to a triple overlap]
77	  \label{lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport}
78	  \uses{def:modules_pullbackComp, def:gr_the_glue_data}
79	  Let \(D\) be a glue datum of schemes (\cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}) with charts
80	  \(U_i\), overlaps \(V_{ij}\), overlap immersions \(f_{ij} : V_{ij} \hookrightarrow
81	  U_i\), transitions \(t_{ij} : V_{ij} \xrightarrow{\sim} V_{ji}\), and triple-overlap
82	  data \(t'_{ijk}\) with its compatibility identity \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\). For an index
83	  triple \((i,j,k)\) write
84	  \[
85	    V_{ijk} \;=\; V_{ij} \times_{U_i} V_{ik}
86	  \]
87	  for the common triple overlap, with the two projections
88	  \(p^{ij}_{ijk} : V_{ijk} \to V_{ij}\) and \(p^{ik}_{ijk} : V_{ijk} \to V_{ik}\), and
89	  let \(t'_{ijk} : V_{ijk} \xrightarrow{\sim} V_{jki}\) be the glue datum's triple
90	  transition, satisfying
91	  \[
92	    t'_{ijk} \,\circ\, p^{jk}_{jki} \;=\; p^{ij}_{ijk} \,\circ\, t_{ij}
93	    \qquad (t_{\mathrm{fac}}).
94	  \]
95	  Given per-chart sheaves of modules \(\mathcal{M}_i\) and a transition isomorphism
96	  \[
97	    g_{ij} \;:\; f_{ij}^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_i
98	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
99	    (t_{ij} \circ f_{ji})^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_j
100	  \]
101	  on the overlap \(V_{ij}\) (as in \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue}), the
102	  \emph{base-change transport} of \(g_{ij}\) to the triple overlap \(V_{ijk}\) is the
103	  isomorphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_{V_{ijk}}\)-modules
104	  \[
105	    \widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}
106	      \;:\;
107	    (f_{ij} \circ p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_i
108	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
109	    (t_{ij} \circ f_{ji} \circ p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_j
110	  \]
111	  obtained by pulling \(g_{ij}\) back along \(p^{ij}_{ijk}\) and reassociating the
112	  iterated pullbacks via \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}. With the analogous transports
113	  \(\widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i}\) and \(\widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j}\) (the first taken along
114	  \(t'_{ijk}\), so that \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\) identifies its source and target sheaves
115	  with those of \(\widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\) and \(\widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j}\)), all three
116	  transported isomorphisms become endomorphism-composable: \(\widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\)
117	  and \(\widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i}\) land on a common sheaf, and their composite is
118	  comparable to \(\widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j}\) on \(V_{ijk}\).
119	\end{lemma}
120	
121	\begin{proof}
122	  \uses{def:modules_pullbackComp, def:gr_the_glue_data}
123	  The pullback functor on sheaves of modules is a pseudofunctor: for composable
124	  scheme morphisms it carries the comparison isomorphisms of
125	  \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}, natural in the sheaf and coherent for triple
126	  composites. Pulling the transition isomorphism \(g_{ij}\) back along the projection
127	  \(p^{ij}_{ijk} : V_{ijk} \to V_{ij}\) yields an isomorphism between
128	  \((p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*} f_{ij}^{*}\mathcal{M}_i\) and \((p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*}(t_{ij} \circ
129	  f_{ji})^{*}\mathcal{M}_j\); applying \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp} to each side
130	  reassociates these into the single-pullback form
131	  \((f_{ij} \circ p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*}\mathcal{M}_i\) and
132	  \((t_{ij} \circ f_{ji} \circ p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*}\mathcal{M}_j\), giving
133	  \(\widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\). For the \((j,k)\)-transition the same construction is
134	  applied along the triple transition \(t'_{ijk}\) rather than a bare projection; the
135	  glue datum's compatibility identity \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\),
136	  \(t'_{ijk} \circ p^{jk}_{jki} = p^{ij}_{ijk} \circ t_{ij}\), guarantees that the
137	  source of \(\widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i}\) coincides with the target of
138	  \(\widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\) (both are the pullback of \(\mathcal{M}_j\) along the same
139	  morphism \(V_{ijk} \to U_j\)), so the two are composable as endomorphism-style
140	  isomorphisms over \(V_{ijk}\). The reassociations introduced by
141	  \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp} are coherent (they satisfy the pentagon identity), so
142	  the composite \(\widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i} \circ \widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\) and the third
143	  transport \(\widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j}\) are isomorphisms between the same pair of
144	  sheaves on \(V_{ijk}\), and may be compared directly. This is the module-level
145	  analogue of the scheme-level triangle \(t'_{ijk}\)/\(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\) and is the
146	  shape in which the multiplicative cocycle condition is stated below.
147	\end{proof}

189	      \(g_{ii} = \mathrm{id}\) on \(f_{ii}^{*}\mathcal{M}_i\) (with
190	      \(V_{ii}\) and \(t_{ii}\) the diagonal data of \(D\));
191	    \item[(C2)] \emph{triple-overlap multiplicativity:} over each common triple
192	      overlap \(V_{ijk} = V_{ij} \times_{U_i} V_{ik}\) the base-change transports
193	      (\cref{lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport}) of the three transition
194	      isomorphisms satisfy
195	      \[
196	        \widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i} \circ \widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}
197	          \;=\; \widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j},
198	      \]
199	      where \(\widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\), \(\widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i}\),
200	      \(\widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j}\) are the transports of \(g_{ij}\), \(g_{jk}\),
201	      \(g_{ik}\) to \(V_{ijk}\) through the scheme-level triple transition
202	      \(t'_{ijk}\) and its compatibility \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\), reassociated by the
203	      pullback comparison \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp} so that all three land on
204	      the same sheaf -- the multiplicative, \(\mathrm{GL}_d\)-valued cocycle
205	      condition.
206	  \end{enumerate}
207	  Conditions (C1) and (C2) are exactly the descent datum for the Zariski cover
208	  \(\{\iota_i\}\); it is subject to them that the gluing below is well-defined and
209	  the glued sheaf is characterised up to unique isomorphism. Out of this data the
210	  construction produces:
211	  \begin{enumerate}
212	    \item a glued sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{M}\) on the glued scheme \(X\); and
213	    \item for each \(i \in J\) a \emph{restriction isomorphism}
214	      \(\rho_i : \iota_i^{*}\,\mathcal{M} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{M}_i\)
215	      identifying the pullback of \(\mathcal{M}\) along \(\iota_i\) with the
216	      \(i\)-th chart sheaf, the \(\rho_i\) being compatible with the transition
217	      isomorphisms \(g_{ij}\) over the overlaps.
218	  \end{enumerate}
219	  The pair \((\mathcal{M}, (\rho_i)_i)\) is characterised up to unique
220	  isomorphism by the restriction property: \(\mathcal{M}\) is the unique sheaf of
221	  modules on \(X\) (up to canonical isomorphism) restricting to each
222	  \(\mathcal{M}_i\) compatibly with \((g_{ij})\). The same primitive descends
223	  morphisms: given two such glued data \((\mathcal{M}_i, g_{ij})\) and
224	  \((\mathcal{N}_i, h_{ij})\) with glued sheaves \(\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}\), and
225	  a family of \(\mathcal{O}_{U_i}\)-linear morphisms
226	  \(\phi_i : \mathcal{M}_i \to \mathcal{N}_i\) that commute with the transition
227	  isomorphisms on every overlap, there is a unique morphism
228	  \(\phi : \mathcal{M} \to \mathcal{N}\) of sheaves of modules on \(X\) with
229	  \(\rho_i^{\mathcal{N}} \circ \iota_i^{*}\phi = \phi_i \circ \rho_i^{\mathcal{M}}\)
230	  for every \(i\) -- the descent of \(\mathrm{Hom}\) along the cover.
231	
232	  \emph{Construction.} A sheaf of modules on \(X\) is equivalent to a compatible
233	  family of sheaves on the members of an open cover of \(X\) together with
234	  identifications on the overlaps -- effective descent for the Zariski cover
235	  \(\{\iota_i : U_i \hookrightarrow X\}\). The charts \(U_i\), being the images of
236	  the glue datum's objects, form such a cover, with intersections
237	  \(U_i \cap U_j\) identified with the overlaps \(V_{ij}\) via the structural data
238	  of \(D\). Pulling the sections of an abstract \(\mathcal{M}\) back to each chart
239	  must reproduce \(\mathcal{M}_i\); the transition isomorphisms \(g_{ij}\),
240	  satisfying the cocycle conditions, are exactly the descent datum that makes the
241	  family \((\mathcal{M}_i)\) glue, so the descent produces \(\mathcal{M}\) with
242	  the asserted restriction isomorphisms \(\rho_i\). The morphism statement is the
243	  descent of morphisms for the same cover: a morphism on \(X\) is a family of
244	  morphisms on the charts agreeing on overlaps, and the compatibility of the
245	  \(\phi_i\) with \((g_{ij}, h_{ij})\) is precisely the agreement condition,
246	  giving the unique glued \(\phi\). This specialises the general descent of sheaves
247	  of modules to the concrete open cover supplied by a scheme glue datum,
248	  and is the form in which the Grassmannian's tautological bundle and quotient are
249	  built.
250	
251	  \emph{Realisation (construction path).} Concretely, instantiate \(D\) as the
252	  Grassmannian glue datum (\cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}), with glued scheme
253	  \(X = \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) and its canonical open cover by the charts
254	  \(\iota_i : U_i \hookrightarrow X\). The glued sheaf \(\mathcal{M}\) is built from
255	  the per-chart sheaves \(\mathcal{M}_i\) and the overlap transition isomorphisms
256	  \(g_{ij}\) satisfying the module cocycle conditions, and is pinned down by the
257	  restriction isomorphisms \(\rho_i : \iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M} \xrightarrow{\sim}
258	  \mathcal{M}_i\) compatible with the \(g_{ij}\). For an open immersion
259	  \(\iota_i : U_i \hookrightarrow X\), the pullback \(\iota_i^{*}\) restricts sheaves
260	  of modules on \(X\) to sheaves of modules on \(U_i\). The transition isomorphisms
261	  \(g_{ij}\), forming a cocycle for this cover, constitute a descent datum; gluing the
262	  local sections yields \(\mathcal{M}\) together with the restriction isomorphisms
263	  \(\rho_i\), where both the assembly and the characterisation up to unique isomorphism
264	  rest on the locality of sections of a sheaf -- a compatible family of sections over
265	  the cover, agreeing on the overlaps, extends to a unique global section.
266	
267	  The morphism-descent half is the same locality argument applied to homomorphisms:
268	  a family \(\phi_i : \mathcal{M}_i \to \mathcal{N}_i\) commuting with the transition
269	  isomorphisms over every overlap is a compatible family of local morphisms, and the
270	  uniqueness of gluing of sections promotes it to a unique global
271	  \(\phi : \mathcal{M} \to \mathcal{N}\) of sheaves of modules on \(X\) restricting
272	  to each \(\phi_i\) through the \(\rho_i\).
273	\end{definition}

275	\section{The tautological quotient on the charts}
276	\label{sec:grquot_chart}
277	
278	\emph{Infrastructure: scalar endomorphisms.} Realising a matrix of regular
279	functions as a morphism of free sheaves of modules has no off-the-shelf primitive
280	in Mathlib; it is assembled from two small project-local helpers that turn a global
281	function into a scalar endomorphism of the structure sheaf, after which the matrix
282	is reconstituted entry-by-entry over a finite biproduct.
283	
284	\begin{definition}\leanok
285	[Global section of the unit module]
286	  \label{def:gr_globalUnitSection}
287	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.globalUnitSection}
288	  \uses{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
289	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(a \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) a global section of
290	  the structure sheaf. Viewing \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as the unit module
291	  \(\mathbf{1} \in \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) (\cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}), the
292	  section \(a\) determines a global section of \(\mathbf{1}\): on each open
293	  \(V \subseteq X\) take the restriction \(a|_V \in \Gamma(V, \mathcal{O}_X)\), the
294	  family \((a|_V)_V\) being compatible with the restriction maps because
295	  restriction is functorial. This is the section of the unit sheaf attached to a
296	  global function.
297	\end{definition}
298	
299	\begin{definition}\leanok
300	[Scalar endomorphism of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)]
301	  \label{def:gr_scalarEnd}
302	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scalarEnd}
303	  \uses{def:gr_globalUnitSection, lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
304	  For \(a \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) the \emph{scalar endomorphism}
305	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(a) : \mathcal{O}_X \to \mathcal{O}_X\) is the
306	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear endomorphism of the unit module given by multiplication
307	  by \(a\). It is obtained from the global section of \cref{def:gr_globalUnitSection}
308	  under the canonical identification of endomorphisms of the unit module
309	  \(\mathbf{1}\) with its global sections, \(\End(\mathbf{1}) \cong
310	  \Gamma(X, \mathbf{1})\); concretely, on each open \(V\) it acts as
311	  multiplication by \(a|_V\). These scalar endomorphisms are the matrix-entry
312	  building blocks of the chart quotient.
313	\end{definition}
314	
315	\begin{lemma}[Scalar endomorphism of the unit is the identity]
316	  \label{lem:gr_scalarEnd_one}
317	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scalarEnd_one}
318	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
319	  The scalar endomorphism attached to the unit function \(1 \in
320	  \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) is the identity of the unit module:
321	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(1) = \mathrm{id}_{\mathbf{1}}\).
322	\end{lemma}
323	
324	\begin{proof}
325	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
326	  Multiplication by \(1\) is the identity on every section, so under the
327	  identification \(\End(\mathbf{1}) \cong \Gamma(X, \mathbf{1})\) the section \(1\)
328	  corresponds to \(\mathrm{id}_{\mathbf{1}}\).
329	\end{proof}
330	
331	\begin{lemma}[Scalar endomorphism of the zero function is zero]
332	  \label{lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero}
333	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scalarEnd_zero}
334	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
335	  The scalar endomorphism attached to the zero function \(0 \in
336	  \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) is the zero endomorphism:
337	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(0) = 0\).
338	\end{lemma}
339	
340	\begin{proof}
341	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
342	  Multiplication by \(0\) sends every section to \(0\), so the corresponding
343	  endomorphism of the unit module is the zero map.
344	\end{proof}
345	
346	\begin{definition}
347	
348	\leanok
349	[Chart quotient \(u^I\)]
350	  \label{def:gr_chart_quotient}
351	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartQuotientMap}
352	  \uses{def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_scalarEnd}
353	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
354	  % sub-section "Universal quotient" (read from
355	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L898-L910).
356	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
357	  % "We next define a rank $d$ locally free sheaf $\U$ on $\grass(r,d)$
358	  %  together with a surjective homomorphism $\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$.
359	  %  On each $U^I$ we define a surjective homomorphism
360	  %  $u^I :\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I} \to  \oplus^d\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I}$
361	  %  by the matrix $X^I$."
362	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
363	  Let \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\). On the affine chart
364	  \(U^I = \Spec R^I\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) define the
365	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}\)-linear homomorphism of trivial bundles
366	  \[
367	    u^I \;:\; \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}
368	      \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}
369	  \]
370	  given by left multiplication by the universal \(d \times r\) matrix \(X^I\)
371	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}): on global sections it is the \(R^I\)-linear
372	  map \((R^I)^{r} \to (R^I)^{d}\), \(v \mapsto X^I v\). Since the \(I\)-th minor
373	  \(X^I_I\) of \(X^I\) is the identity matrix \(1_{d \times d}\), the composite of
374	  \(u^I\) with the inclusion of the \(I\)-indexed coordinates
375	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d} \hookrightarrow \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\) is the
376	  identity; hence \(u^I\) is a split surjection of locally free sheaves, with
377	  target the free rank-\(d\) sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\).
378	
379	  \emph{Realisation.} The free sheaves \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\) and
380	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\) are the finite biproducts (coproducts) of copies of
381	  the unit module \(\mathbf{1} = \mathcal{O}_{U^I}\), indexed by \(\{1,\dots,r\}\)
382	  and \(\{1,\dots,d\}\). Each entry \(X^I_{p,q} \in R^I\) of the universal matrix
383	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}) is a global function on \(U^I = \Spec R^I\),
384	  obtained from \(R^I\) through the canonical identification
385	  \(\Gamma(\Spec R^I, \mathcal{O}) \cong R^I\); the corresponding scalar
386	  endomorphism \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(X^I_{p,q}) : \mathbf{1} \to \mathbf{1}\)
387	  (\cref{def:gr_scalarEnd}) is the \((p,q)\)-component of \(u^I\). Assembling these
388	  components over the index biproducts -- the universal property of the biproduct
389	  packaging a matrix of component morphisms into a single morphism of the free
390	  sheaves -- yields \(u^I\). This entry-by-entry assembly via the finite biproduct
391	  is the substitute for a (non-existent) Mathlib ``matrix \(\mapsto\) morphism of
392	  free sheaves'' primitive.
393	\end{definition}
394	
395	\begin{lemma}[The $I$-columns of the chart quotient are the identity]
396	  \label{lem:gr_chartQuotientMap_iFree}
397	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartQuotientMap_ιFree}
398	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_scalarEnd,
399	    lem:gr_scalarEnd_one, lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero,
400	    lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}
401	  For each \(k \in \{1,\dots,d\}\), the chart quotient \(u^I\)
402	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}) carries the \(\iota_I(k)\)-th basis section of the
403	  free sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\) -- where \(\iota_I(k)\) is the \(k\)-th
404	  element of \(I\) in increasing order -- to the \(k\)-th basis section of
405	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\); that is, the \(I\)-indexed columns of \(u^I\) form the
406	  identity:
407	  \[
408	    e_{\iota_I(k)} \,\circ\, u^I \;=\; e_k.
409	  \]
410	\end{lemma}
411	
412	\begin{proof}
413	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_scalarEnd,
414	    lem:gr_scalarEnd_one, lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero,
415	    lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}
416	  By the biproduct presentation of \(u^I\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}), the
417	  composite \(e_{\iota_I(k)} \circ u^I\) reads off the \(\iota_I(k)\)-th column of the
418	  component matrix, whose \(p\)-th entry is the scalar endomorphism
419	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(X^I_{p,\iota_I(k)})\). The \(I\)-indexed columns of the
420	  universal matrix \(X^I\), read through the order isomorphism \(\{1,\dots,d\} \cong
421	  I\), form the \(d \times d\) identity (\cref{lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}),
422	  so \(X^I_{p,\iota_I(k)} = \delta_{p,k}\). Hence the diagonal entry is
423	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(1) = \mathrm{id}\) (\cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_one}) and the
424	  off-diagonal entries are \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(0) = 0\)
425	  (\cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero}); the resulting column is the \(k\)-th coordinate
426	  inclusion, giving \(e_{\iota_I(k)} \circ u^I = e_k\).
427	\end{proof}
428	
429	\begin{lemma}\leanok
430	[The chart quotient is an epimorphism]
431	  \label{lem:gr_chartQuotientMap_epi}
432	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartQuotientMap_epi}
433	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient}
434	  The chart quotient \(u^I : \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\)
435	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}) is an epimorphism of sheaves of modules.
436	\end{lemma}
437	
438	\begin{proof}
439	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient, lem:gr_chartQuotientMap_iFree}
440	  We exhibit a section, so that \(u^I\) is a \emph{split} epimorphism and hence an
441	  epimorphism. Let \(\iota_I : \{1,\dots,d\} \hookrightarrow \{1,\dots,r\}\) be the
442	  inclusion of the \(I\)-indexed coordinates, \(k \mapsto \iota_I(k)\), the
443	  \(k\)-th element of \(I\) in increasing order. It induces a morphism of free
444	  sheaves \(s_I : \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d} \to \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\), the
445	  coordinate inclusion sending the \(k\)-th basis section to the
446	  \(\iota_I(k)\)-th. We claim
447	  \[
448	    u^I \circ s_I \;=\; \mathrm{id}_{\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}}.
449	  \]
450	  Both sides are morphisms out of the free rank-\(d\) sheaf, which is the coproduct
451	  of \(d\) copies of the unit module; by the universal property of that coproduct
452	  it suffices to check the equality on each coordinate, i.e.\ to compare component
453	  matrices. The \((p,k)\)-component of \(u^I \circ s_I\) is the entry
454	  \(X^I_{p,\iota_I(k)}\) of the universal matrix at the \(I\)-indexed column, which
455	  is precisely the \(I\)-th minor \(X^I_I\) of \(X^I\). Since that minor is the
456	  \(d \times d\) identity (the columns of \(X^I\) indexed by \(I\), read through the
457	  order isomorphism \(\{1,\dots,d\} \cong I\), form the identity block --
458	  \cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}), one has \(X^I_{p,\iota_I(k)} = \delta_{p,k}\),
459	  so \(u^I \circ s_I\) has the identity component matrix and equals
460	  \(\mathrm{id}\). Hence \(s_I\) splits \(u^I\) and \(u^I\) is a split
461	  epimorphism, in particular an epimorphism.
462	\end{proof}
463	
464	\begin{definition}
465	
466	\leanok
467	[The universal quotient sheaf \(\mathcal{U}\)]
468	  \label{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}
469	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalQuotient}
470	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_universalMinorInv, def:gr_transition,
471	    lem:gr_cocycle, def:gr_glued_scheme, def:scheme_modules_glue,
472	    def:is_locally_free_of_rank}
473	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
474	  % sub-section "Universal quotient" (read from
475	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L903-L910).
476	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
477	  % "Compatible with the co-cycle $(\theta_{I,J})$
478	  %  for gluing the affine pieces $U^I$, we give gluing data
479	  %  $(g_{I,J})$ for gluing together the trivial bundles $\oplus^d\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I}$
480	  %  by putting
481	  %  $$g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1} \in GL_d(U^I_J)$$"
482	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
483	  Over each overlap \(U^I_J = \Spec R^I_J\) define the bundle-transition matrix
484	  \[
485	    g_{I,J} \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} \;\in\; \mathrm{GL}_d(R^I_J),
486	  \]
487	  the Cramer inverse of the localised \(J\)-minor (\cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}),
488	  which is invertible because \(\det(X^I_J)\) is a unit of \(R^I_J\). The matrices
489	  \((g_{I,J})\) are compatible with the chart-gluing cocycle \((\theta_{I,J})\)
490	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{lem:gr_cocycle}): over a triple overlap they
491	  satisfy \(g_{I,K} = g_{J,K}\, g_{I,J}\) and \(g_{I,I} = 1\), the multiplicative
492	  cocycle condition for a vector bundle. Gluing the free rank-\(d\) sheaves
493	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\) along the isomorphisms \(g_{I,J}\) over the cover
494	  \(\{U^I\}\) of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) therefore
495	  produces a locally free \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}\)-module
496	  \(\mathcal{U}\) of rank \(d\). Its determinant line bundle \(\det \mathcal{U}\)
497	  has transition functions \(\det(g_{I,J}) = 1/P^I_J\), the data underlying the
498	  Pl\"ucker embedding.
499	\end{definition}

501	\begin{definition}
502	
503	\leanok
504	[The tautological quotient \(u : \mathcal{O}^{r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\)]
505	  \label{def:tautological_quotient}
506	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.tautologicalQuotient}
507	  \uses{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf, def:gr_chart_quotient,
508	    def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_universalMinorInv, lem:gr_cocycle,
509	    def:scheme_modules_glue}
510	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
511	  % sub-section "Universal quotient" (read from
512	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L908-L910).
513	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
514	  % "This is compatible with the homomorphisms $u^I$,
515	  %  so we get a surjective homomorphism
516	  %  $u : \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$."
517	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
518	  The chart quotients \(u^I : \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\)
519	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}) are compatible with the bundle gluing
520	  \((g_{I,J})\) of \(\mathcal{U}\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}): over
521	  each overlap \(U^I_J\) one has the identity of \(d \times r\) matrices
522	  \[
523	    g_{I,J}\, X^I \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I \;=\; X^J \qquad \text{on } U^I_J,
524	  \]
525	  which is precisely the defining formula of the chart transition
526	  \(\theta_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}).
527	  Hence the local maps \(u^I\), transported through the trivialisations of
528	  \(\mathcal{U}\), agree on overlaps and glue to a single surjective
529	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}\)-linear homomorphism
530	  \[
531	    u \;:\; \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\,r}
532	      \;\twoheadrightarrow\; \mathcal{U},
533	  \]
534	  the \emph{tautological} (or \emph{universal}) rank-\(d\) quotient of the trivial
535	  rank-\(r\) sheaf. It is surjective because each \(u^I\) is (split) surjective and
536	  surjectivity is local on \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\), and \(\mathcal{U}\) is locally
537	  free of rank \(d\) by \cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}. The pair
538	  \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\) is the family of rank-\(d\) quotients carried
539	  by \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) itself.
540	\end{definition}

542	\section{The functor of points}
543	\label{sec:grquot_functor}
544	
545	\begin{definition}
546	
547	\leanok
548	[The Grassmannian / Quot-of-trivial-sheaf functor \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\)]
549	  \label{def:grassmannian_functor}
550	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.functor}
551	  \uses{def:is_locally_free_of_rank, def:quot_functor}
552	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
553	  % Exercise (2) "Grassmannian as a Quot scheme" (read from
554	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L557-L564).
555	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
556	  % "represents the
557	  %  contravariant functor
558	  %  $$\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$$
559	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any $T$ the
560	  %  set of all equivalence classes
561	  %  $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients $q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F$
562	  %  where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$."
563	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2).}
564	  The \emph{Grassmannian functor}
565	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d) = \Quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}
566	  \mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/\mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) is the contravariant functor
567	  from schemes to sets defined as follows. For a scheme \(T\), a \emph{family of
568	  rank-\(d\) quotients of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\)} is a pair
569	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) consisting of a locally free
570	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-module \(\mathcal{F}\) of rank \(d\) together with a surjective
571	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-linear homomorphism \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}
572	  \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\). Two such families
573	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) and \(\langle \mathcal{F}', q' \rangle\) are
574	  \emph{equivalent} if there is an isomorphism \(f : \mathcal{F}
575	  \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{F}'\) with \(f \circ q = q'\); equivalently
576	  \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\) as subsheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\). Set
577	  \[
578	    \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T)
579	      \;=\; \bigl\{\, \text{equivalence classes } \langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle
580	        \text{ of rank-}d\text{ quotients of } \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r} \,\bigr\}.
581	  \]
582	  For a morphism \(\varphi : T' \to T\), the map
583	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(\varphi)\) sends \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\)
584	  to the pulled-back quotient \(\langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{F},\, \varphi^{*}q
585	  \rangle\); this is well-defined on equivalence classes because pullback is right
586	  exact and preserves local freeness of rank \(d\). (As \(\mathcal{F}\) is locally
587	  free of rank \(d\), surjectivity is preserved and the support is automatically
588	  proper -- all of \(T'\) -- so this is the special case
589	  \(E = \mathcal{O}^{r}\), \(X = S = \operatorname{base}\) of the general Quot
590	  functor of \cref{def:quot_functor}.)
591	\end{definition}

593	\section{The universal property}
594	\label{sec:grquot_universal}
595	
596	\begin{theorem}
597	
598	\leanok
599	[\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) represents the Grassmannian functor]
600	  \label{thm:grassmannian_universal_property}
601	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.represents}
602	  \uses{def:grassmannian_functor, def:tautological_quotient,
603	    def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf, def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_affine_chart,
604	    def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle}
605	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
606	  % Exercise (2) "Grassmannian as a Quot scheme" (read from
607	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L546-L566).
608	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
609	  % "Show that $\grass(r,d)$ together with the quotient
610	  %  $u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$ represents the
611	  %  contravariant functor
612	  %  $$\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$$
613	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any $T$ the
614	  %  set of all equivalence classes
615	  %  $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients $q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F$
616	  %  where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$.
617	  %  Therefore, $\quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$
618	  %  exists, and equals $\grass(r,d)$."
619	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2).}
620	  The Grassmannian scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}),
621	  together with the tautological quotient \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\)
622	  (\cref{def:tautological_quotient}), represents the functor
623	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:grassmannian_functor}). That is, for
624	  every scheme \(T\) the natural map
625	  \[
626	    \operatorname{Hom}(T, \mathrm{Gr}(r,d))
627	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
628	    \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T),
629	    \qquad
630	    \bigl(\,\varphi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\,\bigr)
631	      \;\longmapsto\;
632	    \langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U},\, \varphi^{*}u \rangle,
633	  \]
634	  is a bijection, natural in \(T\). Consequently the Quot scheme
635	  \(\quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/
636	  \mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) of rank-\(d\) quotients of the trivial rank-\(r\) sheaf
637	  exists and equals \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\); the tautological quotient is the
638	  universal family.
639	\end{theorem}
640	
641	\begin{proof}
642	  \uses{def:tautological_quotient, def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf,
643	    def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_universal_matrix,
644	    def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle, def:gr_glued_scheme,
645	    def:grassmannian_functor}
646	  Naturality of \(\varphi \mapsto \langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U}, \varphi^{*}u
647	  \rangle\) is immediate: pullback is functorial and \(\varphi^{*}u\) is again a
648	  surjection onto a rank-\(d\) locally free sheaf, so the assignment defines a
649	  morphism of functors \(\operatorname{Hom}(-,\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)) \to
650	  \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\). It remains to show it is a bijection for each
651	  \(T\); we exhibit an inverse, constructed Zariski-locally on \(T\) and glued.
652	
653	  \emph{From a quotient to a morphism.} Let \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle \in
654	  \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T)\), so \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r} \twoheadrightarrow
655	  \mathcal{F}\) with \(\mathcal{F}\) locally free of rank \(d\). For a size-\(d\)
656	  subset \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) let \(T_I \subseteq T\) be the locus where
657	  the composite
658	  \[
659	    \mathcal{O}_T^{\,d}
660	      \overset{\ \iota_I\ }{\hookrightarrow} \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}
661	      \xrightarrow{\ q\ } \mathcal{F},
662	  \]
663	  with \(\iota_I\) the inclusion of the \(I\)-indexed coordinates, is an
664	  isomorphism. This locus is open: in a local trivialisation of \(\mathcal{F}\)
665	  the composite is a \(d \times d\) matrix and \(T_I\) is the non-vanishing locus
666	  of its determinant. The \(T_I\) cover \(T\): at any point \(t\), the
667	  \(d\)-dimensional fibre quotient \(q(t) : \kappa(t)^{r} \twoheadrightarrow
668	  \mathcal{F}\otimes\kappa(t)\) is surjective, so some \(d\) of the \(r\) standard
669	  basis vectors map to a basis of the \(d\)-dimensional fibre, i.e.\ \(t \in T_I\)
670	  for that \(I\) (by Nakayama the isomorphism then holds in a neighbourhood).
671	
672	  On \(T_I\), trivialise \(\mathcal{F}\) using \(\iota_I^{*}\): the quotient
673	  becomes a surjection \(q_I : \mathcal{O}_{T_I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{T_I}^{\,d}\)
674	  represented by a \(d \times r\) matrix \(M^I\) whose \(I\)-th minor \(M^I_I\) is
675	  the identity \(1_{d \times d}\) -- exactly the shape of the universal matrix
676	  \(X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}). The \(d(r-d)\) free entries of \(M^I\)
677	  are global functions on \(T_I\) and hence determine a unique
678	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra homomorphism \(R^I = \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to
679	  \Gamma(T_I, \mathcal{O}_{T_I})\) sending \(x^I_{p,q}\) to the corresponding entry
680	  of \(M^I\), i.e.\ a unique morphism \(\varphi_I : T_I \to U^I\)
681	  (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) with \(\varphi_I^{*}X^I = M^I\) and thus
682	  \(\varphi_I^{*}u^I = q_I\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}).
683	
684	  \emph{Gluing.} On the overlap \(T_I \cap T_J\) the two trivialisations differ by
685	  the change-of-basis \((M^I_J)^{-1}\), which is exactly the bundle transition
686	  \(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1}\) of \(\mathcal{U}\)
687	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}); concretely
688	  \(M^J = (M^I_J)^{-1} M^I = \varphi_I^{*}\theta_{I,J}(X^J)\)
689	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}). Hence
690	  \(\varphi_I\) and \(\varphi_J\) land in the overlap charts and agree there after
691	  the transition \(\theta_{I,J}\), so by the cocycle condition
692	  (\cref{lem:gr_cocycle}) the \(\varphi_I\) glue to a single morphism
693	  \(\varphi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}). By
694	  construction \(\varphi^{*}\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle = \langle \mathcal{F},
695	  q \rangle\), since this holds on each \(T_I\) and the equivalence class is
696	  determined by \(\ker(q)\).
697	
698	  \emph{Uniqueness.} If \(\psi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) also satisfies
699	  \(\psi^{*}\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\), then
700	  on \(\psi^{-1}(U^I)\) the pulled-back tautological quotient \(\psi^{*}u^I\)
701	  equals \(q\) in the \(I\)-trivialisation, which forces \(\psi^{*}X^I = M^I\) and
702	  hence \(\psi = \varphi_I\) there; as the \(U^I\) cover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\),
703	  \(\psi = \varphi\). Thus \(\varphi\) is the unique morphism inducing
704	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\), and \(\varphi \mapsto \langle
705	  \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U}, \varphi^{*}u \rangle\) is bijective.
706	
707	  Therefore \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) with \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\) is a fine
708	  moduli space: it represents \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\), so
709	  \(\quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/
710	  \mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) exists and equals \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\).
711	\end{proof}

994	  Let \(e : M \xrightarrow{\sim} N\) be an additive equivalence and let \(M\) carry an
995	  \(R\)-module structure. Pulling the action back along \(e\) by
996	  \(r \cdot y := e\big(r \cdot e^{-1}(y)\big)\) makes \(N\) an \(R\)-module for which \(e\)

998	  \(R\) then so is \(N\); and (b) if \(M\) additionally carries a compatible action of a
999	  base ring \(A_g\) with scalar tower \(A_g \to R \to M\), then the pulled-back actions on
1000	  \(N\) again form a scalar tower \(A_g \to R \to N\).

1005	  by construction \(e\) is then an \(R\)-linear isomorphism. Statement (a) follows by
1006	  transporting module-finiteness across the \(R\)-linear isomorphism \(e\).
1007	  Statement (b) checks the scalar-tower identity
1008	  \((a\,r)\cdot y = a\cdot(r\cdot y)\) on \(N\) by transporting it across \(e\) and applying
1009	  associativity of the scalar action on \(M\).

1094	  in \(A_g\)), so \(\bar a = h\,\bar s\) is associated to \(h\); in particular \(\bar a \ne 0\)
1095	  (because \(h \ne 0\)), hence \(a \ne 0\). Since \(A\) is a domain and \(g, a \ne 0\), the
1096	  product \(f := g\,a\) is non-zero.
1097	
1098	  \emph{Identify the base rings.} The associatedness of \(\bar a\) and \(h\), together with the
1099	  tower \(A \to A_g \to A_h\), identifies the iterated localisation
1100	  \((A_g)_h = \mathrm{Away}(h)\) with the single localisation of \(A\) away from the product
1101	  \(g\,a\): by \cref{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated} (with \(x = g\), \(z = a\),
1102	  \(y = h\)) the ring \(A_h\) is a localisation of \(A\) away from \(g\,a\), and uniqueness of
1103	  localisation furnishes a ring isomorphism \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a) \xrightarrow{\sim} A_h\).
1104	
1105	  \emph{Identify the modules.} The composite localisation map
1106	  \[
1107	    \psi : T \longrightarrow (T_g)_h, \qquad
1108	    \psi := \mathrm{mk}_{\langle h\rangle} \circ \mathrm{mk}_{\langle g\rangle},
1109	  \]
1110	  first into \(T_g\) and then into \((T_g)_h\), realises \((T_g)_h\) as the localisation of
1111	  \(T\) at the powers of \(g\,a\): every \(g^n a^m\) acts invertibly on \((T_g)_h\), \(\psi\) is
1112	  surjective up to such denominators, and its kernel is the expected one. This
1113	  localisation-of-localisation identity is verified directly from the defining properties of a
1114	  localised module; no packaged lemma supplies it.
1115	
1116	  \emph{Transport freeness.} By hypothesis \((T_g)_h\) is free over \(A_h\). The ring
1117	  isomorphism \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a) \cong A_h\) and the induced semilinear module equivalence
1118	  \(T_{g\,a} \simeq (T_g)_h\) — assembled from the localised-module identification of the
1119	  previous step and promoted to an \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a)\)-linear equivalence — carry this
1120	  freeness back by \cref{lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}: \(T_{g\,a}\) is free over
1121	  \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a)\). Taking \(f := g\,a\) gives \(T_f\) free over \(A_f\), as required.
1122	\end{proof}

1146	  the polynomial ring \(A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\), regarded as an \(A\)-module. Then there
1147	  exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
1148	
1149	\end{lemma}

1151	\begin{proof}
1152	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module, lem:gf_splice_shortExact, lem:gf_torsion_base,
1153	        lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport, lem:gf_generic_rank_ses,
1154	        lem:gf_torsion_reindex, lem:gf_away_tower_descent}
1155	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1760-L1772).
1156	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (polynomial-ring core).}
1157	  We argue by strong induction on \(d\), \emph{generalising the base domain \(A\)} (along
1158	  with the module \(N\)): the inductive hypothesis is available for every \(d' < d\),
1159	  every noetherian domain in place of \(A\), and every finite module over the
1160	  corresponding polynomial ring.
1161	
1162	  \emph{Base case \(d = 0\).} The ring \(P_0 = A[X_1, \dots, X_0]\) is \(A\) itself, so
1163	  \(N\) is a finite \(A\)-module and the claim is exactly the finite-module leaf
1164	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_module}.
1165	
1166	  \emph{Inductive step \(d \ge 1\).} If \(N\) is already a torsion \(P_d\)-module over
1167	  \(A\) — equivalently \(N_K = 0\) for \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\) — then the torsion base
1168	  case \cref{lem:gf_torsion_base} produces \(f \ne 0\) with \(N_f = 0\), which is free
1169	  over \(A_f\). Otherwise we proceed in five steps.
1170	  \begin{enumerate}
1171	    \item \textbf{Generic-rank SES.} Apply \cref{lem:gf_generic_rank_ses} to \(N\) over
1172	      \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\): there are \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) and an injective
1173	      \(P_d\)-linear map \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) with torsion cokernel
1174	      \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\), i.e.\ a short exact sequence of
1175	      \(P_d\)-modules
1176	      \[
1177	        0 \to P_d^{\oplus m} \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } N \to T \to 0,
1178	        \qquad T \text{ torsion}.
1179	      \]
1180	      No element of \(A\) is inverted at this step.
1181	    \item \textbf{Reindex the torsion cokernel.} Apply \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex} to the
1182	      finite torsion module \(T\): there are \(g \ne 0\) in \(A\) and \(m' < d\) such that
1183	      the localisation \(T_g\) is module-finite over the polynomial ring
1184	      \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\) in fewer variables over the base ring \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\).
1185	    \item \textbf{Inductive hypothesis at the new base \(A_g\).} Since \(A_g\) is again a
1186	      noetherian domain and \(m' < d\), the inductive hypothesis applies \emph{at base
1187	      \(A_g\)} to the finite \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\)-module \(T_g\): there is
1188	      \(h \ne 0\) in \(A_g\) with \((T_g)_h\) free over \((A_g)_h\). This is exactly the
1189	      point at which the base ring of the induction must be allowed to change from \(A\)
1190	      to \(A_g\).
1191	    \item \textbf{Descend the witness from \(A_g\) to \(A\).} Clearing the denominator of
1192	      \(h\) over \(A_g\) produces a numerator \(a \in A\) whose image in \(A_g\) is associated
1193	      to \(h\); then the iterated localisation \((A_g)_h\) is the single localisation
1194	      \(A_{g a}\) and \((T_g)_h\) is \(T_{g a}\). Thus freeness of \((T_g)_h\) over
1195	      \((A_g)_h\) transports to freeness of \(T_{g a}\) over \(A_{g a}\) by the
1196	      iterated-\(\mathrm{Away}\)-tower descent \cref{lem:gf_away_tower_descent}. Set
1197	      \(f := g a \ne 0\) — the single product, \emph{not} a square — so that \(T_f\) is free
1198	      over \(A_f\).
1199	    \item \textbf{Splice.} The left end \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) of the sequence is a free
1200	      \(A\)-module (the polynomial ring \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) is \(A\)-free, hence
1201	      so is any finite direct power; note \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) is \emph{not} module-finite
1202	      over \(A\), but freeness is all the splice requires), and a free module stays free
1203	      under the localisation \(A \to A_f\); so \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) localises to a free
1204	      \(A_f\)-module. With both ends free over \(A_f\) after inverting \(f = g a\),
1205	      \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact} splices the localised short exact sequence to
1206	      conclude that the middle term \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\). Taking \(f = g a\)
1207	      completes the induction.
1208	  \end{enumerate}
1209	\end{proof}

1255	\subsection{Geometric bridges for the affine assembly}
1256	\label{sec:gf_geometric_bridges}
1257	
1258	The geometric form of generic flatness is assembled from the algebraic core by
1259	restricting to a non-empty affine open of \(S\) and working patchwise over a finite
1260	affine cover of the preimage. Two genuinely geometric steps are isolated here as
1261	named bridges: the affine-local finiteness of the section module of a finite-type
1262	quasi-coherent sheaf (G1), feeding the algebraic core its finite-module input; and
1263	the flat-locality assembly (G3), promoting per-patch freeness to flatness of the
1264	restricted sheaf over the base open.

1349	\subsection{Transport engine for generating sections}
1350	
1351	The restriction-of-generators lemma below is assembled from a generic transport
1352	principle: a finite generating family of a sheaf of modules \(\mathcal{M}\) pushes
1353	forward to a finite generating family of \(F(\mathcal{M})\) along any functor \(F\) of
1354	sheaves of modules that preserves colimits and carries the structure sheaf to the
1355	structure sheaf. The three blocks of this subsection package that principle (the
1356	generating family, its index type, and the finiteness bookkeeping). They are
1357	project-bespoke: Mathlib provides the analogous transport only for full presentations
1358	(with kernel data), not for bare generating families.

1367	  source to that of the target. Given a generating family \(\sigma\) of \(\mathcal{M}\)
1368	  with index set \(I = \sigma.I\) and epimorphism
1369	  \(\sigma.\pi : \OO_R^{\oplus I} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{M}\), the images
1370	  \((\mathrm{mapFree}\,F\,\eta\,I)^{-1} \circ F(\sigma.\pi)\) form a generating family of
1371	  \(F(\mathcal{M})\) with the same index set \(I\). The map is an epimorphism because it
1372	  is the composite of an isomorphism with \(F(\sigma.\pi)\), and \(F\) preserves the
1373	  epimorphism \(\sigma.\pi\).

1453	\begin{lemma}[Half 2, seam 1c — finite generation equals a finite free epimorphism]
1454	  \label{lem:gf_finite_gen_iff_free_epi}
1455	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_finite_gen_iff_free_epi}
1456	  Let \(Y\) be an open of a scheme and \(\F\) a sheaf of modules on \(Y\). Then
1457	  \(\F\) is globally generated by a finite family of sections if and only if there is a
1458	  finite index set \(I\) and an epimorphism of \(\OO_Y\)-modules
1459	  \(\OO_Y^{\oplus I} \twoheadrightarrow \F\). No quasi-coherence of \(\F\) is assumed:
1460	  the statement holds for arbitrary sheaves of modules on \(Y\), and applies in particular
1461	  to the restrictions \(\F|_{D(g)}\) arising in the cover assembly.
1462	\end{lemma}

1464	\begin{proof}
1465	  A finite family of sections \(\{s_i\}_{i \in I}\) of \(\F|_Y\) is the same datum as an
1466	  \(\OO_Y\)-linear map \(\OO_Y^{\oplus I} \to \F|_Y\) sending the \(i\)-th basis section to
1467	  \(s_i\). The family generates \(\F|_Y\) (the induced map is surjective on every stalk)
1468	  exactly when this map is an epimorphism of sheaves of modules. Thus a finite generating
1469	  family yields a finite-free epimorphism, and conversely the images of the basis sections
1470	  of any epimorphism \(\OO_Y^{\oplus I} \twoheadrightarrow \F|_Y\) with \(I\) finite form a
1471	  finite generating family.
1472	\end{proof}
